{"parentUuid":"b35ad911-b55c-45f0-a368-9147b9008efc","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"e4b3ce19-89dd-409d-b0c7-935fedb62227","agentId":"a2af46c92d818d1a3","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_011KtdJLbHGSZ9zWXaeFZvFA","type":"tool_result","content":"# GeoJSON Files in historical-basemaps/geojson\n\nHere are all 54 GeoJSON files available in this directory:\n\n**Ancient Period (BC):**\nworld_bc123000.geojson, world_bc10000.geojson, world_bc8000.geojson, world_bc5000.geojson, world_bc4000.geojson, world_bc3000.geojson, world_bc2000.geojson, world_bc1500.geojson, world_bc1000.geojson, world_bc700.geojson, world_bc500.geojson, world_bc400.geojson, world_bc323.geojson, world_bc300.geojson, world_bc200.geojson, world_bc100.geojson, world_bc1.geojson\n\n**Common Era:**\nworld_100.geojson, world_200.geojson, world_300.geojson, world_400.geojson, world_500.geojson, world_600.geojson, world_700.geojson, world_800.geojson, world_900.geojson, world_1000.geojson, world_1100.geojson, world_1200.geojson, world_1279.geojson, world_1300.geojson, world_1400.geojson, world_1492.geojson, world_1500.geojson, world_1530.geojson, world_1600.geojson, world_1650.geojson, world_1700.geojson, world_1715.geojson, world_1783.geojson, world_1800.geojson, world_1815.geojson, world_1880.geojson, world_1900.geojson, world_1914.geojson, world_1920.geojson, world_1930.geojson, world_1938.geojson, world_1945.geojson, world_1960.geojson, world_1994.geojson, world_2000.geojson, world_2010.geojson\n\n**Additional:**\nplaces.geojson"}]},"uuid":"d3381849-bb01-4849-8934-afd173463450","timestamp":"2026-03-24T20:36:56.829Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"b35ad911-b55c-45f0-a368-9147b9008efc","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"fbbc3797-9e93-4bbd-b200-e1d46e5b4e6a","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"e4b3ce19-89dd-409d-b0c7-935fedb62227","agentId":"a2af46c92d818d1a3","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01VTNugDAu1inYM1ffFxHeXA","type":"tool_result","content":"Web search results for query: \"Clio-Infra polity2 index data download political entities historical\"\n\nLinks: [{\"title\":\"Polity2 Index | Clio Infra | Reconstructing Global Inequality\",\"url\":\"https://clio-infra.eu/Indicators/Polity2Index.html\"},{\"title\":\"Polity2 Index - Clio Infra\",\"url\":\"https://datasets.iisg.amsterdam/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=hdl:10622/HOPFWK\"},{\"title\":\"Clio Infra | Reconstructing Global Inequality\",\"url\":\"https://clio-infra.eu/\"},{\"title\":\"Polyarchy | Clio Infra | Reconstructing Global Inequality\",\"url\":\"https://clio-infra.eu/Indicators/Polyarchy.html\"},{\"title\":\"Political Competition | Clio Infra | Reconstructing Global Inequality\",\"url\":\"https://clio-infra.eu/Indicators/PoliticalCompetition.html\"},{\"title\":\"Polity 5 — download_polity_annual • democracyData\",\"url\":\"https://xmarquez.github.io/democracyData/reference/download_polity_annual.html\"},{\"title\":\"Polity data series - Wikipedia\",\"url\":\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polity_data_series\"},{\"title\":\"Polity 2 from PMM — polity_pmm • democracyData\",\"url\":\"https://xmarquez.github.io/democracyData/reference/polity_pmm.html\"},{\"title\":\"GitHub - open-numbers/ddf--clio_infra--indicators\",\"url\":\"https://github.com/open-numbers/ddf--clio_infra--indicators\"},{\"title\":\"Polity Data Archive // Kristian Skrede Gleditsch\",\"url\":\"http://ksgleditsch.com/polity.html\"}]\n\nBased on the search results, here's what I found about the Clio-Infra Polity2 Index data:\n\n## Overview\n\nThe Polity2 Index is an autocracy-democracy index ranging between -10 (total autocracy) and 10 (total democracy) from the Polity IV dataset, created by Monty G. Marshall, Keith Jaggers, and Ted Robert Gurr.\n\n## Access and Download\n\nThe data is available through the Clio Infra project hosted by the International Institute of Social History (IISH), which has set up interconnected databases containing worldwide data on social, economic, and institutional indicators for the past five centuries, with special attention to the past 200 years.\n\nYou can access the Polity2 Index specifically at the Clio-Infra website at https://clio-infra.eu/Indicators/Polity2Index.html, and the data is also available through a dataset repository at https://datasets.iisg.amsterdam/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=hdl:10622/HOPFWK.\n\n## Data Coverage\n\nThe project provides historical datasets for modern 2012 boundaries, and for available datasets with historical boundaries there are additional options available.\n\n## Related Resources\n\nThe polity2_index is included in the Clio Infra indicators available through GitHub, along with other political indicators, which provides another access point for the data in a structured format.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."}]},"uuid":"475d8519-7e27-441b-b341-d062b57b0464","timestamp":"2026-03-24T20:36:59.244Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"fbbc3797-9e93-4bbd-b200-e1d46e5b4e6a","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"475d8519-7e27-441b-b341-d062b57b0464","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"a2af46c92d818d1a3","message":{"model":"claude-opus-4-6","id":"msg_012pJUsVX8rXPrYcVNNZ93yG","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Now I have comprehensive information. Let me compile the full response.\n\n---\n\nHere is a comprehensive survey of additional databases and datasets for tracking historical polities, borders, and territorial changes, beyond the ones you already know about.\n\n---\n\n## 1. POLITY V / POLITY IV (Center for Systemic Peace)\n\n- **URL**: [https://www.systemicpeace.org/polityproject.html](https://www.systemicpeace.org/polityproject.html)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 1800-2018\n- **Spatial coverage**: All independent states with >500,000 population\n- **Data format**: XLS, CSV (downloadable)\n- **Territorial relevance**: **Limited for your purposes.** Polity V codes regime characteristics (autocracy-democracy scores), not territorial boundaries. However, it does include a \"polity fragmentation\" variable that codes whether a separate polity exists within recognized borders, and whether a state exercises effective authority over at least 50% of its territory. It does NOT contain shapefiles or border geometries. It uses COW country codes and has start/end dates for each polity, making it useful as a temporal roster of political entities rather than a spatial dataset.\n\nSources: [Polity V Manual (PDF)](https://www.systemicpeace.org/inscr/p5manualv2018.pdf), [Princeton DSS Catalog](https://dss.princeton.edu/catalog/resource93), [Polity data series - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polity_data_series)\n\n---\n\n## 2. Clio-Infra\n\n- **URL**: [https://clio-infra.eu/](https://clio-infra.eu/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 1500-2018 (86 datasets, 224 countries)\n- **Spatial coverage**: Global, keyed to modern 2012 boundaries\n- **Data format**: CSV (downloadable per indicator), also on [IISG Dataverse](https://datasets.iisg.amsterdam/) and [GitHub](https://github.com/open-numbers/ddf--clio_infra--indicators)\n- **Territorial relevance**: Clio-Infra is primarily a socioeconomic indicator database (GDP, population, wages, education, etc.), NOT a border/territory database. It does include a **Polity2 Index** indicator (the autocracy-democracy score from Polity IV) and political competition indices. All data is mapped to modern country boundaries, so it does not directly track historical border changes. However, its country-year panel structure (with notes on boundary adjustments) can be useful as a reference for which entities existed when.\n\nSources: [Clio-Infra Portal](https://clio-infra.eu/), [Polity2 Index at Clio-Infra](https://clio-infra.eu/Indicators/Polity2Index.html)\n\n---\n\n## 3. World Historical Gazetteer (WHG)\n\n- **URL**: [https://whgazetteer.org/](https://whgazetteer.org/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: All centuries since 1500 (core spine), with some data going much further back\n- **Spatial coverage**: Global -- over 2 million place records, 70+ published datasets\n- **Data format**: Linked Places format (JSON-LD); datasets downloadable under CC BY 4.0; also on [Harvard Dataverse](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataverse/WHG)\n- **Territorial relevance**: WHG is a gazetteer, not a border dataset, but it includes names of polities, districts, and significant settlements with temporal attributes. It integrates data from Getty TGN (~1.8M records), GeoNames, D-Place, the Atlas of World History, and HGIS de las Indias (~15,000 settlements/territories in colonial Latin America). Useful for place-name resolution and linking polity names to locations over time, though it does not provide polygon boundaries for polities.\n\nSources: [WHG Public Data](https://whgazetteer.org/public_data/), [WHG Harvard Dataverse](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataverse/WHG), [Programming Historian Tutorial](https://programminghistorian.org/en/lessons/finding-places-world-historical-gazetteer)\n\n---\n\n## 4. Historical International Political Economy (HIPE)\n\n- **URL**: The LSE reference at [https://researchonline.lse.ac.uk/122429/](https://researchonline.lse.ac.uk/122429/) is a chapter by J. Morrison in *The Oxford Handbook of International Political Economy* (2021) -- it is a scholarly review article about how IPE scholars use history, NOT a dataset.\n- **Territorial relevance**: **This does not appear to be a downloadable dataset.** The closest actual dataset is the **International Political Economy Data Resource** (see below).\n\n### The International Political Economy (IPE) Data Resource (Graham & Tucker)\n\n- **URL**: [Harvard Dataverse](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/X093TV)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 1800-2018 (varies by variable)\n- **Spatial coverage**: 200+ countries\n- **Data format**: CSV, Stata (.dta)\n- **Territorial relevance**: Merges 951 variables from 78 IPE data sources into a single country-year panel. Includes COW codes, Gleditsch-Ward codes, and ISO codes. Does not contain border geometries but provides a comprehensive concordance of country identifiers across time, which is very useful for linking datasets.\n\nSources: [IPE Data Resource on Harvard Dataverse](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/X093TV), [Niehaus Center at Princeton](https://niehaus.princeton.edu/data), [Graham & Tucker paper](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11558-017-9285-0)\n\n---\n\n## 5. Aourednik Historical Basemaps\n\n- **URL**: [https://github.com/aourednik/historical-basemaps](https://github.com/aourednik/historical-basemaps)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 123,000 BC to 2010 CE (54 GeoJSON snapshots)\n- **Spatial coverage**: Global\n- **Data format**: GeoJSON (primary), SVG; WGS 84 / EPSG:4326\n- **Territorial relevance**: **Highly relevant.** Provides ready-to-use world political boundary polygons at specific time snapshots. Key snapshots within your 1860-1960 window: **1880, 1900, 1914, 1920, 1930, 1938, 1945, 1960**. Each polygon includes NAME, SUBJECTO (colonial authority), PARTOF, and BORDERPRECISION (1-3 accuracy rating). Open source, community-maintained. **Caveat**: Work in progress; verify against other sources before academic use.\n\nSources: [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/aourednik/historical-basemaps), [README](https://github.com/aourednik/historical-basemaps/blob/master/README.md)\n\n---\n\n## 6. Thenmap\n\n- **URL**: [https://www.thenmap.net/](https://www.thenmap.net/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: Varies by dataset. World borders: 1945-present. US states: 1865-present. Swedish/Norwegian/Finnish/Danish/Swiss municipalities: various modern periods.\n- **Spatial coverage**: Global (world borders), plus country-specific subnational datasets\n- **Data format**: GeoJSON, TopoJSON, SVG via REST API; also JavaScript and Python libraries\n- **Territorial relevance**: Provides an API where you query a date (YYYY-MM-DD) and get back borders that existed on that exact date. Uses ISO 3166 codes and integrates with Wikidata for metadata. Good for programmatic access to post-1945 world borders. Limited coverage before 1945 for the world dataset. API endpoint: `http://api.thenmap.net/`\n\nSources: [Thenmap website](https://www.thenmap.net/), [API v2 Documentation](http://api.thenmap.net/doc/v2/), [J++ Blog Post](https://jplusplus.org/en/blog/version-two-of-the-thenmap-api/)\n\n---\n\n## 7. Euratlas Georeferenced Historical Vector Data\n\n- **URL**: [https://www.euratlas.net/shop/](https://www.euratlas.net/shop/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: Year 1 CE to 2000 CE, at 100-year intervals (centennial snapshots)\n- **Spatial coverage**: Europe (15W to 50E, 20N to 60N)\n- **Data format**: Shapefiles (ESRI)\n- **Territorial relevance**: **Very relevant for European polities.** Each century includes layers for sovereign states, autonomous peoples, cities (with population), rivers, seas, and \"fuzzy_borders\" polygons indicating boundary uncertainty. Covers your 1860-1960 window at the 1900 and 2000 snapshots. **Caveat**: Commercial product (paid download). Some versions accessible free through [Duke University Libraries](https://library.duke.edu/data/sources/euratlas) for Duke affiliates.\n\nSources: [Euratlas Shop](https://www.euratlas.net/shop/maps_gis/gis_1900.html), [Duke University Libraries](https://library.duke.edu/data/sources/euratlas)\n\n---\n\n## 8. MPIDR Census Mosaic / Population History GIS Collection (Max Planck Institute for Demographic Research)\n\n- **URL**: [https://mosaic.ipums.org/historical-gis-datafiles](https://mosaic.ipums.org/historical-gis-datafiles) (now hosted at IPUMS)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 1837-2003, varies by country\n- **Spatial coverage**: Europe (Germany, Austria-Hungary, Serbia, Albania, and pan-European overview)\n- **Data format**: Shapefiles (ESRI), free for non-commercial scientific use (registration required)\n- **Territorial relevance**: **Directly relevant for European territories 1860-1960.** Key datasets:\n  - **European administrative boundaries** at 30-year intervals (1900, 1930, 1960, 1990, 2003)\n  - **German Empire** small administrative areas (annual resolution)\n  - **Austro-Hungarian Empire** court districts (1910)\n  - **Serbia** district-level boundaries\n  - **Prussia** (1860-1870) geocodes based on Galloway's statistical data\n\nSources: [Census Mosaic GIS Files](https://mosaic.ipums.org/historical-gis-datafiles), [Censusmosaic at Berkeley](https://censusmosaic.demog.berkeley.edu/data/historical-gis-files)\n\n---\n\n## 9. IPUMS IHGIS (International Historical Geographic Information System)\n\n- **URL**: [https://ihgis.ipums.org/](https://ihgis.ipums.org/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: Varies by country; many censuses from mid-1800s onward\n- **Spatial coverage**: Global (census data from many countries)\n- **Data format**: CSV data tables + Shapefiles for administrative boundaries (boundaries aligned to U.S. State Department LSIB)\n- **Territorial relevance**: Provides historical census boundary shapefiles for countries worldwide. While primarily designed for census data linkage, the GIS boundary files themselves represent historical administrative divisions at the time of each census. Useful for tracking how internal administrative units changed over time.\n\nSources: [IHGIS Main Page](https://ihgis.ipums.org/), [IHGIS Geography & GIS](https://ihgis.ipums.org/geography-gis)\n\n---\n\n## 10. COW Territorial Change Dataset v6\n\n- **URL**: [https://correlatesofwar.org/data-sets/territorial-change/](https://correlatesofwar.org/data-sets/territorial-change/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 1816-2018\n- **Spatial coverage**: Global (all territorial changes involving at least one nation-state)\n- **Data format**: CSV, XLS\n- **Territorial relevance**: **Highly relevant as a structured event log.** Each record documents a territorial transfer: gaining entity, losing entity, territory exchanged, type of exchange (conquest, cession, secession, unification, mandate, etc.), and date. Does NOT include shapefiles/geometries -- it is a tabular dataset, not a spatial one. But it is the most comprehensive catalogue of \"when did territory X change hands\" for the 1816-2018 period.\n\nSources: [COW Territorial Change](https://correlatesofwar.org/data-sets/territorial-change/), [Tir, Diehl, and Goertz (1998)](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/247713936_Territorial_Changes_1816-1996_Procedures_and_Data)\n\n---\n\n## 11. International Border Agreements Dataset (IBAD) -- Owsiak et al.\n\n- **URL**: [https://www.andrewowsiak.org/international-border-agreements-dataset.html](https://www.andrewowsiak.org/international-border-agreements-dataset.html)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 1816-2001\n- **Spatial coverage**: Global (all dyadic interstate borders)\n- **Data format**: CSV; also on [Harvard Dataverse](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/AJVSJR)\n- **Territorial relevance**: Records whether neighboring states have delimited their mutual border under international law, the method used (negotiation, mediation, arbitration, adjudication, plebiscite, post-war conference), and the outcome. Useful for understanding which borders in your 1860-1960 window were formally agreed vs. contested.\n\nSources: [Owsiak website](https://www.andrewowsiak.org/international-border-agreements-dataset.html), [Harvard Dataverse](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/AJVSJR)\n\n---\n\n## 12. V-Dem (Varieties of Democracy)\n\n- **URL**: [https://www.v-dem.net/data/](https://www.v-dem.net/data/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 1789-present (202 countries; Historical V-Dem covers ~80 polities back to 1789)\n- **Spatial coverage**: Global\n- **Data format**: CSV, Stata, R package\n- **Territorial relevance**: V-Dem codes regime characteristics for \"political units enjoying at least some degree of functional and/or formal sovereignty.\" Historical V-Dem includes **defunct polities** such as Bavaria and the Two Sicilies. It tracks name changes, territorial changes, colonization, and independence events. Does NOT include border geometries, but provides the most comprehensive temporal roster of sovereign and semi-sovereign entities with start/end dates, useful as a concordance layer.\n\nSources: [V-Dem Dataset Page](https://www.v-dem.net/data/the-v-dem-dataset/), [Historical V-Dem](https://www.v-dem.net/about/historical-v-dem/)\n\n---\n\n## 13. GeoEPR (Geo-referencing Ethnic Power Relations) -- ETH Zurich\n\n- **URL**: [https://icr.ethz.ch/data/epr/geoepr/](https://icr.ethz.ch/data/epr/geoepr/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 1946-2021\n- **Spatial coverage**: Global\n- **Data format**: ESRI Shapefile, CSV, JSON, Excel\n- **Territorial relevance**: Provides polygon geometries for politically relevant ethnic groups, not state borders per se. However, it includes settlement pattern polygons that can indicate de facto territorial control by non-state or sub-state groups. Useful as a complementary layer showing how territories were actually controlled vs. formally claimed.\n\nSources: [GeoEPR 2021](https://icr.ethz.ch/data/epr/geoepr/), [ETH ICR Data Portal](https://icr.ethz.ch/data/)\n\n---\n\n## 14. From Empire to Nation-State Dataset (Wimmer & Min)\n\n- **URL**: [https://www.awimmer.com/data](https://www.awimmer.com/data) and [Columbia description (PDF)](http://www.columbia.edu/~aw2951/EmpireNSdataset.pdf)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 1816-2001\n- **Spatial coverage**: 156 territorial units globally\n- **Data format**: Stata, CSV\n- **Territorial relevance**: **Directly relevant.** A cross-territory panel dataset using the 2001 grid of states, tracking the entire history of each territory back to 1816 including years under imperial control. Records which empire controlled each territory, when nation-state formation occurred, and war incidence. Covers 140 territories that were incorporated into an empire at some point, and 150 that experienced nation-state creation. Excellent for your 1860-1960 period of decolonization and imperial transitions.\n\nSources: [Wimmer Data Page](https://www.awimmer.com/data), [Wimmer & Min 2006 paper](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/000312240607100601)\n\n---\n\n## 15. China Historical GIS (CHGIS) -- Harvard\n\n- **URL**: [https://chgis.fas.harvard.edu/](https://chgis.fas.harvard.edu/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 221 BCE to 1911 CE (dynastic period), plus 1820 and 1911 nationwide snapshots\n- **Spatial coverage**: China\n- **Data format**: Shapefiles, XML; on [Harvard Dataverse](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataverse/chgis_v6)\n- **Territorial relevance**: Provides boundary shapefiles for Chinese provinces, circuits, prefectures, and counties as they changed over time through the dynastic period. Free for academic use. The 1911 snapshot is relevant for the early part of your 1860-1960 window.\n\nSources: [CHGIS Main Site](https://chgis.fas.harvard.edu/), [CHGIS Dataverse](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataverse/chgis_v6)\n\n---\n\n## 16. HGIS de las Indias\n\n- **URL**: [https://hgis-indias.net/](https://hgis-indias.net/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 1701-1808\n- **Spatial coverage**: Colonial Spanish America\n- **Data format**: Vector data (shapefiles), interactive WebGIS; on [Harvard Dataverse](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataverse/hgis-indias)\n- **Territorial relevance**: Maps colonial administrative units (viceroyalties, judicial districts, provinces, dioceses) year by year. Includes a gazetteer of 12,000+ populated places and 1,000+ territorial entities. Predates your 1860-1960 window but relevant for understanding colonial territorial structures in Latin America.\n\nSources: [HGIS de las Indias](https://hgis-indias.net/index.php/en/), [Harvard Dataverse](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataverse/hgis-indias)\n\n---\n\n## 17. Atlas of Historical County Boundaries (Newberry Library)\n\n- **URL**: [https://publications.newberry.org/ahcb/](https://publications.newberry.org/ahcb/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: States/territories 1783-2000; counties 1629-2000\n- **Spatial coverage**: United States\n- **Data format**: Shapefiles, KMZ (Google Earth)\n- **Territorial relevance**: The most detailed dataset for U.S. internal boundary changes. Every county boundary change is recorded. Fully covers your 1860-1960 window for the U.S.\n\nSources: [AHCB Downloads](https://publications.newberry.org/ahcb/downloads/united_states.html)\n\n---\n\n## 18. ISO 3166-3 (Formerly Used Country Codes)\n\n- **URL**: [https://www.iso.org/iso-3166-country-codes.html](https://www.iso.org/iso-3166-country-codes.html)\n- **Additional resources**: [Statoids change history](https://statoids.com/w3166his.html), [Luke's ISO-3166 GitHub repo](https://github.com/lukes/ISO-3166-Countries-with-Regional-Codes), [DataHub country-codes](https://datahub.io/core/country-codes)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 1974-present (codes assigned since ISO 3166 was first published)\n- **Spatial coverage**: Global\n- **Data format**: CSV, JSON, XML\n- **Territorial relevance**: ISO 3166-3 defines codes for country names deleted from ISO 3166-1 (e.g., Yugoslavia, Czechoslovakia, USSR). The Statoids site provides detailed change histories with withdrawal dates. The `countrycode` R package and `democracyData` package provide concordance tables mapping ISO, COW, and GW codes across time. Useful for building a temporal lookup of which codes were valid when, but does not go back before 1974 and does not include geometries.\n\nSources: [ISO 3166 Official](https://www.iso.org/iso-3166-country-codes.html), [Statoids](https://statoids.com/w3166his.html), [countrycode R package](https://rdrr.io/github/vincentarelbundock/countrycode/man/codelist.html), [democracyData package](https://xmarquez.github.io/democracyData/reference/country_year_coder.html)\n\n---\n\n## 19. LSIB (Large Scale International Boundaries) -- U.S. State Department\n\n- **URL**: [https://geonode.state.gov/layers/geonode%3ALSIB](https://geonode.state.gov/layers/geonode%3ALSIB)\n- **Temporal coverage**: Current (v11.4, February 2025); not historical\n- **Spatial coverage**: Global\n- **Data format**: Shapefile, WMS; downloadable from [data.gov](https://catalog.data.gov/dataset/large-scale-international-boundaries)\n- **Territorial relevance**: The official U.S. Government international boundary lines. Current only -- no historical snapshots. But it is the authoritative reference boundary used by IHGIS and other projects to align their historical shapefiles to a common modern baseline.\n\nSources: [LSIB on Data.gov](https://catalog.data.gov/dataset/large-scale-international-boundaries), [State Dept GeoNode](https://geonode.state.gov/layers/geonode%3ALSIB)\n\n---\n\n## 20. OpenAtlas\n\n- **URL**: [https://openatlas.eu/](https://openatlas.eu/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: Variable (user-created databases)\n- **Spatial coverage**: Variable\n- **Data format**: Open source web application (Python/Flask, PostgreSQL/PostGIS); data modeled with CIDOC CRM ontology\n- **Territorial relevance**: OpenAtlas is a platform/framework, not a pre-built dataset. It is used by archaeological and historical research projects to create structured databases of places, events, and actors with spatial and temporal attributes. Relevant if you want to build your own historical polity database with a standards-compliant data model.\n\nSources: [OpenAtlas website](https://openatlas.eu/), [GitHub](https://github.com/acdh-oeaw/OpenAtlas)\n\n---\n\n## 21. Chronas\n\n- **URL**: [https://chronas.org/](https://chronas.org/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: Claims 50+ million interconnected data points across history\n- **Spatial coverage**: Global\n- **Data format**: Web application; **no direct GeoJSON/shapefile export capability**\n- **Territorial relevance**: An interactive history map visualization tool. While visually impressive, it does not offer data downloads in standard GIS formats, limiting its utility for research pipelines.\n\nSources: [Chronas website](https://chronas.org/)\n\n---\n\n## 22. A Vision of Britain through Time / Great Britain Historical GIS\n\n- **URL**: [https://www.visionofbritain.org.uk/](https://www.visionofbritain.org.uk/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 1801-present (primarily since the first UK census)\n- **Spatial coverage**: England, Wales, Scotland\n- **Data format**: Digital boundaries for registration districts, local government districts, and parishes; available as shapefiles\n- **Territorial relevance**: The most detailed historical boundary dataset for Britain. Covers registration districts and poor law unions (c.1840-1911), local government districts (1911-1974), and parishes (1870s-1974). Directly covers your 1860-1960 window at sub-national level.\n\nSources: [Vision of Britain](https://www.visionofbritain.org.uk/), [Great Britain Historical GIS - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Britain_Historical_GIS)\n\n---\n\n## 23. Harvard Dataverse: Additional Relevant Datasets\n\nSeveral individually relevant datasets on Harvard Dataverse:\n\n- **Shaping States into Nations** (ethnic geography and European state borders since 1855): [DOI:10.7910/DVN/T1NXVQ](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/T1NXVQ)\n- **Endogenous Colonial Borders** (precolonial states and the partition of Africa): [DOI:10.7910/DVN/9QJVJ1](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9QJVJ1)\n- **Princes and Townspeople** (historical statistics on German territories and cities with border maps): [DOI:10.7910/DVN/ZGSJED](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZGSJED)\n- **World-Historical Dataverse** (curated list of datasets): [DOI:10.7910/DVN/29089](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/29089)\n\n---\n\n## 24. GeaCron\n\n- **URL**: [http://geacron.com/](http://geacron.com/)\n- **Temporal coverage**: 3000 BCE to present\n- **Spatial coverage**: Global\n- **Data format**: Web-only visualization; **no GIS data export**\n- **Territorial relevance**: An interactive world history atlas. No polygon extraction capability, so not usable in a data pipeline. For visualization/reference only.\n\nSources: [GeaCron website](http://geacron.com/home-en/)\n\n---\n\n## Summary: Best Picks for Your 1860-1960 Focus\n\n| Dataset | Has Geometries | 1860-1960 Coverage | Downloadable | Format |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| **Aourednik Historical Basemaps** | Yes | 1880, 1900, 1914, 1920, 1930, 1938, 1945, 1960 | Yes (free) | GeoJSON |\n| **MPIDR Census Mosaic** | Yes | 1900, 1930, 1960 (Europe) | Yes (free, registration) | Shapefile |\n| **Euratlas** | Yes | 1900, 2000 snapshots | Paid | Shapefile |\n| **COW Territorial Change v6** | No (tabular) | 1816-2018 (full coverage) | Yes (free) | CSV |\n| **Wimmer & Min Empire-to-Nation** | No (tabular) | 1816-2001 (full coverage) | Yes (free) | CSV/Stata |\n| **IBAD (Owsiak)** | No (tabular) | 1816-2001 | Yes (free) | CSV |\n| **V-Dem Historical** | No (tabular) | 1789-present | Yes (free) | CSV/Stata |\n| **Thenmap API** | Yes | 1945-present (world) | Yes (free API) | GeoJSON/TopoJSON |\n| **IHGIS (IPUMS)** | Yes | Varies by country census | Yes (free, registration) | Shapefile + CSV |\n\nThe **Aourednik Historical Basemaps** combined with the **COW Territorial Change v6** tabular data likely give you the best combination of spatial geometries and structured event data for the 1860-1960
